app.subtitle: Multilingual disaster-prevention info, leaving no one behind

home.heroTitle: Be ready for disasters — the map for everyone
home.heroSubtitle: A multilingual disaster-prevention portal for living safely in Japan: earthquakes, tsunamis, typhoons and more
home.situationBody: Japan sits where four tectonic plates meet and is home to many of the world's active volcanoes. People live alongside earthquakes, tsunamis, volcanic eruptions, typhoons and heavy rain. The right knowledge and everyday preparation save lives when disaster strikes.
home.featureMapDesc: Find the nearest shelters on the map
home.featureKnowledgeDesc: Learn about earthquakes, tsunamis, volcanoes and how to prepare
home.featureAiDesc: Ask about disaster prevention in plain language

knowledge.heading: Disaster Info
knowledge.lead: Based on official sources such as the Prime Minister’s Office, JMA and MLIT, this page summarizes preparation, evacuation decisions and disaster-specific actions.

preparedness.name: Everyday preparation
preparedness.summary: Disasters can interrupt electricity, gas, water, communications and transport. Prepare at home and work so you can protect lives and live until help arrives.
tips: Anchor furniture and keep tall items away from bedrooms and children’s rooms
Store at least three days of drinking water, ideally one week, using 3 liters per person per day as a guide
Keep your emergency bag light enough to carry while walking
actions: Keep a flashlight, slippers, whistle, glasses and regular medicine near where you sleep
Prepare portable toilets, batteries, a radio, medicine records, ID copies and a mobile battery
Agree on multiple family contact methods such as Disaster Message Dial 171 and message boards
Use hazard maps to check flood, landslide, tsunami and storm-surge risks around home, school and work

evacuation.name: Evacuation decisions
evacuation.summary: Evacuation does not only mean going to a public shelter. Choose a place that protects life according to the disaster type, location and family situation.
tips: At Alert Level 3, older people and others who need time should start evacuating
At Alert Level 4 evacuation order, everyone in a dangerous place should evacuate
Do not wait for Alert Level 5; act before dark and before wind or rain becomes severe
actions: Consider several destinations: designated emergency sites, safe relatives’ homes, hotels or upper floors
Sheltering indoors is only suitable when the building is safe and you can stay above expected flood depth
Avoid rivers, channels, coasts, underpasses, cliffs and slopes while evacuating
Check shelter openings, phone numbers and road closures only through municipal official sources

earthquake.name: Earthquake
earthquake.summary: When shaking starts or an earthquake early warning is issued, protect yourself first. Avoid injury before trying to handle fire sources.
tips: Get low, protect your head and stay under a sturdy table until shaking stops
Do not rush outside; falling glass, walls and blocks can be dangerous
Do not force yourself to turn off fire sources during strong shaking
actions: After shaking stops, check for fire, gas smell, injuries, broken glass and exits
Open doors or windows to secure an evacuation route and wear shoes or slippers
Near the coast or river mouths, evacuate to high ground after strong or long shaking without waiting
In stations or commercial facilities, do not rush to exits; follow staff instructions and avoid elevators

tsunami.name: Tsunami
tsunami.summary: A tsunami is a powerful movement of the whole body of seawater. Later waves may be higher, so do not return until warnings are lifted.
tips: If you feel strong or long shaking near the coast or a river mouth, move to high ground immediately
When a tsunami warning, advisory or evacuation order is issued, leave beaches, seawalls, river mouths and riversides
Do not go to watch or photograph the tsunami
actions: Check nearby high ground, tsunami towers, tsunami evacuation buildings and municipal evacuation sites in advance
Evacuate on foot in principle unless local plans specify vehicle evacuation
Do not return to coastal or flooded areas until tsunami warnings and advisories are lifted
Even after warnings are lifted, watch for damaged roads, debris, electric shock and fire, and follow official instructions

heavyRain.name: Heavy rain, typhoon and flood
heavyRain.summary: Heavy rain and typhoons can cause floods, inland flooding, landslides, strong winds, high waves, storm surges, outages and flooded underground spaces.
tips: Check JMA warnings, Kikikuru risk maps, MLIT river information and municipal evacuation information
Use hazard maps to confirm flood zones, house-collapse flood zones and evacuation routes
Stay away from rivers, channels, coasts, flooded roads, underground malls and underpasses
actions: Before wind and rain worsen, check emergency supplies, shutters, drains and outdoor items
Households with older people, infants, disabled people, pregnant people or pets should evacuate early
If going outside is dangerous, move to a sturdy nearby building or an upper floor
Do not try to pass flooded roads on foot or by car

landslide.name: Landslide
landslide.summary: Slope failures, debris flows and landslides can occur after heavy rain or earthquakes, leaving very little time to escape.
tips: Check whether your home, school route or workplace is in a landslide warning area
Monitor landslide warnings, rainfall, Kikikuru and municipal evacuation information
Do not wait for warning signs; evacuate early when alerts or orders are issued
actions: Watch for falling pebbles, water from slopes, ground cracks, rumbling sounds or muddy rivers
Avoid slopes, valleys, cliffs, bridges and flooded roads when evacuating
If outside evacuation is unsafe, move to the second floor or higher of a sturdy building away from the slope
Do not stay on the first floor of wooden houses or in rooms facing the mountain side

volcano.name: Volcano
volcano.summary: Eruptions can bring volcanic rocks, pyroclastic flows, volcanic mudflows, lava, ash and gases. Large rocks and pyroclastic flows are especially dangerous.
tips: Check volcanic hazard maps, eruption alert levels, access restrictions and municipal evacuation plans
Before hiking, check volcanic information and prepare a helmet, mask, light, water and food
If an eruption alert is issued, move away from the crater and protect your head in a sturdy building or behind rocks
actions: Cover your nose and mouth with a dust mask or wet cloth to avoid inhaling ash
If ash enters your eyes, rinse with water without rubbing and remove contact lenses
Avoid driving during ashfall because visibility and road grip worsen
When cleaning ash, use a mask, goggles and gloves, and follow municipal disposal instructions

multilingual.name: Multilingual official information
multilingual.summary: People who are not confident in Japanese also need fast and accurate information. Use official multilingual and plain-Japanese sources, not guesses or unverified posts.
tips: JMA provides multilingual information on weather, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanoes
The Cabinet Office introduces useful disaster apps and websites in multiple languages
Social media can be old or unverified, so prioritize municipal and public-agency information
actions: Before disasters, show foreign residents local shelters, hazard maps, apps and municipal social media
Use short, concrete messages that say who should do what, where and when
Use action-focused wording such as “If you are near the sea, move to high ground now”
Confirm shelter openings, phone numbers and current local conditions from official municipal sources
